metadata.title: Tunneling: one angstrom, one order of magnitude
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=transmitted/surviving wavefunction; CRIMSON=barrier/forbidden region / blocked; GOLD=log-scale meter readout
metadata.note: Exclusions: this card's angle is exponential-sensitivity/STM framing, not the tunneling setup itself. No WKB derivation. No Gamow factor derivation. Overlaps Vol.1 barrier penetration — anchor to T=e^(-2kL) and log-sensitivity.

beats.narration_text: A scanning tunneling microscope tip sits above a metal surface. Move the tip one angstrom closer — one tenth of a nanometer, the width of a single atom. The tunneling current jumps by a factor of ten.

props.headline: A scanning tunneling microscope tip sits above a metal surface. Move…

card.headline:
Move an STM tip one
angstrom closer.
The current jumps tenfold.

beats.narration_text: Classical physics says the current should change smoothly with distance. But tunneling current drops by a full order of magnitude for every angstrom of added gap. Why is the sensitivity so savage?

props.headline: Classical physics says the current should change smoothly with distance. But…

card.headline:
Classical current changes
smoothly. Tunneling current
collapses by powers of ten.
Why?

beats.narration_text: In a classically forbidden region — where the particle's energy is less than the barrier height — the Schrodinger equation gives a real decaying exponential, not an oscillating wave. The wavefunction falls off as e to the minus kappa times x.

props.headline: In a classically forbidden region — where the particle's energy is…

beats.narration_text: Kappa is the spatial decay rate. For an electron facing a barrier one electron-volt above its energy, kappa works out to about five inverse nanometers. That is one e-fold of decay every point two nanometers.

props.headline: Kappa is the spatial decay rate. For an electron facing a…

beats.narration_text: The transmission probability through a rectangular barrier of width L is T equals e to the minus 2 kappa L. The factor of two comes from squaring the amplitude to get the probability. The exponent is proportional to the width.

props.headline: The transmission probability through a rectangular barrier of width L is…

beats.narration_text: Every 2.3 units of 2 kappa L reduces T by exactly one order of magnitude. That comes from the natural logarithm: e to the minus 2.303 equals one tenth. So adding barrier width does not subtract from T — it divides it.

props.headline: Every 2.3 units of 2 kappa L reduces T by exactly…

beats.narration_text: Plot T versus barrier width on a semi-log scale. It becomes a straight line. A straight line on a semi-log plot means the underlying law is exponential. Each equal step in width gives an equal multiplicative drop in T.

props.headline: Plot T versus barrier width on a semi-log scale. It becomes…

beats.narration_text: For the STM, this savage sensitivity is the feature, not the bug. The tip sits only two or three angstroms from the surface. A single angstrom of height change changes the tunneling current by a factor of e — about 2.7. That is enough contrast to resolve individual atoms.

props.headline: For the STM, this savage sensitivity is the feature, not the…

beats.narration_text: The same exponential sensitivity makes tunneling negligible once the barrier is a few nanometers wide. At 2 kappa L equals 20, T is about two times ten to the minus nine — essentially zero. Quantum tunneling turns off fast.

props.headline: The same exponential sensitivity makes tunneling negligible once the barrier is…

beats.narration_text: An STM tip in vacuum above a copper surface. Barrier height above electron energy: about 4.5 electron-volts. Kappa is roughly 10.8 inverse nanometers. At gap 0.3 nanometers, 2 kappa L equals 6.5, T is about one and a half thousandths. Move the tip 0.1 nanometer closer: 2 kappa L drops to 4.3, T rises about ten-fold. These are illustrative values.

props.headline: An STM tip in vacuum above a copper surface. Barrier height…

beats.narration_text: Tunneling transmission is e to the minus 2 kappa L. Each equal step in barrier width multiplies T by the same factor. One angstrom, one order of magnitude.

props.headline: Tunneling transmission is e to the minus 2 kappa L. Each…

card.headline:
T = e^(-2kL).
One angstrom.
One order of magnitude.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Math for Quantum Physics series.
